title: LLM Engineering
description: Build reliable language-model applications with prompts, structured output, and tools.
outcomes: Explain how LLMs fit into AI products versus full models.
Write prompt patterns aligned to user intent.
Generate structured responses that are stable and parseable.
Use function calling and tool composition safely.
Evaluate prompts and model behavior with repeatable checks.

lessons.title: What is an LLM?
lessons.description: Frame where LLMs help and where they are the wrong tool.
checklist: I can explain LLM strengths and limits in one paragraph.
I can compare LLMs with smaller ML models.
I can identify one unsuitable use case.
questions: When is an LLM overkill for a problem?
What role should product intent play in model choice?

lessons.title: Tokens
lessons.description: Understand token budgets, length, and cost-aware design.
checklist: I can estimate token impact of prompts.
I can reduce prompt verbosity.
I can plan around context constraints.
questions: Why do token budgets matter in production?
How do you cut prompt noise without losing clarity?

lessons.title: Context Windows
lessons.description: Use context windows to keep inputs relevant and safe.
checklist: I can summarize long inputs into compact context.
I can prioritize signals by freshness and trust.
I can manage truncation fallback.
questions: What goes wrong when context exceeds limits?
How do you choose what to drop first?

lessons.title: Prompt Engineering
lessons.description: Build prompt patterns with stable and controllable outputs.
checklist: I can write a role + goal + constraints prompt.
I can include format guidance without over-constraining.
I can test at least two prompt variants.
questions: How do you prevent instruction drift in prompts?
What prompt component reduces ambiguity most?

lessons.title: Structured Outputs
lessons.description: Return typed, machine-readable outputs for product workflows.
checklist: I can define a schema for response shape.
I can parse and validate model output.
I can handle invalid outputs gracefully.
questions: Why are plain-text responses risky for automation?
How do you design a schema before generation?

lessons.title: Function Calling
lessons.description: Let the model trigger safe, typed backend capabilities.
checklist: I can define simple tool signatures.
I can reject unsafe invocations.
I can log tool requests and errors.
questions: Why is schema validation critical in tool calls?
When should tool calls be disabled?

lessons.title: Tool Use
lessons.description: Compose multiple tools into meaningful multi-step flows.
checklist: I can design tool selection logic.
I can chain tools in safe order.
I can recover from tool failures.
questions: What makes tool orchestration more reliable than hardcoded logic?
How do you stop tool loops in production?

lessons.title: Evaluation
lessons.description: Create objective checks for prompt and behavior quality.
checklist: I can define measurable grading criteria.
I can track cost, latency, and quality.
I can set regression tests for behavior.
questions: What metric matters more: latency or quality?
How do you reduce hallucinations operationally?

lessons.title: Mini Project: LLM Routing Checker
lessons.description: Build an end-to-end prompt + tool routing mini workflow.
checklist: I can route input to intent-based actions.
I can return structured outputs reliably.
I can explain failure fallbacks.
questions: How would routing reduce model error?
What is your fallback policy when tools fail?

lessons.title: Portfolio Project: Resume Analyzer
lessons.description: Build an LLM-powered resume analyzer with structure and guardrails.
checklist: I can design prompt contracts with schemas.
I can implement tool-based checks.
I can evaluate behavior across edge inputs.
questions: How do you ensure output consistency at scale?
Which constraints keep the feature trustworthy?

lessons.title: LLM Engineering Quiz
lessons.description: Validate LLM engineering fundamentals before moving to agent workflows.
checklist: I can explain LLM limits in production.
I can use structured output with confidence.
I can justify tooling and evaluation choices.
questions: How do you design LLM flows that can fail safely?
What evaluation habits prevent regression drift?

quizQuestions.prompt: In your own words, explain when an LLM should be chosen over a smaller model.
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Choose an LLM when the task needs flexible natural language understanding or generation, while smaller models are preferred for deterministic, constrained prediction tasks.

options.label: One giant prompt
options.explanation: Long prompts can waste tokens, increase cost, and still fail without structure and evaluation.

options.label: Flexible language tasks
options.explanation: Choose an LLM when the task needs flexible natural language understanding or generation, while smaller models are preferred for deterministic, constrained prediction tasks.

options.label: Trust requested tools
options.explanation: Tool calls need validation, permissions, and error handling before execution.

options.label: Free-form integrations
options.explanation: Automated systems need schemas and validation so outputs can be parsed reliably.

quizQuestions.prompt: How does token budget influence prompt and product design?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Token limits force prioritization and concise prompts so responses stay within context while preserving relevant product intent.

options.label: Trust requested tools
options.explanation: Tool calls need validation, permissions, and error handling before execution.

options.label: Budgeted context design
options.explanation: Token limits force prioritization and concise prompts so responses stay within context while preserving relevant product intent.

options.label: Free-form integrations
options.explanation: Automated systems need schemas and validation so outputs can be parsed reliably.

options.label: Keep all context
options.explanation: Context should be summarized, filtered, and constrained to reduce noise, cost, and privacy risk.

quizQuestions.prompt: How would you keep long conversation context useful and safe?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Summarize older turns, keep high-signal data, and drop stale details while retaining constraints and action-relevant context.

options.label: Free-form integrations
options.explanation: Automated systems need schemas and validation so outputs can be parsed reliably.

options.label: Keep all context
options.explanation: Context should be summarized, filtered, and constrained to reduce noise, cost, and privacy risk.

options.label: Fluency-only evaluation
options.explanation: LLM evaluation should track correctness, usefulness, safety, latency, and cost.

options.label: Summarize and filter context
options.explanation: Summarize older turns, keep high-signal data, and drop stale details while retaining constraints and action-relevant context.

quizQuestions.prompt: Describe a prompt pattern that improves consistency.
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Use a fixed role, objective, format contract, few-shot examples, and explicit constraints.

options.label: Keep all context
options.explanation: Context should be summarized, filtered, and constrained to reduce noise, cost, and privacy risk.

options.label: Role, format, examples, constraints
options.explanation: Use a fixed role, objective, format contract, few-shot examples, and explicit constraints.

options.label: Fluency-only evaluation
options.explanation: LLM evaluation should track correctness, usefulness, safety, latency, and cost.

options.label: LLM for every task
options.explanation: Smaller deterministic or ML systems can be cheaper, faster, and easier to control.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is schema-first design important for automated systems?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Structured outputs make downstream systems parse safely and reduce manual interpretation errors from model responses.

options.label: Fluency-only evaluation
options.explanation: LLM evaluation should track correctness, usefulness, safety, latency, and cost.

options.label: LLM for every task
options.explanation: Smaller deterministic or ML systems can be cheaper, faster, and easier to control.

options.label: One giant prompt
options.explanation: Long prompts can waste tokens, increase cost, and still fail without structure and evaluation.

options.label: Schema-first parsing
options.explanation: Structured outputs make downstream systems parse safely and reduce manual interpretation errors from model responses.

quizQuestions.prompt: Explain why tool call validation is a reliability concern.
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Tool validation prevents unsafe or malformed calls and gives the system an explicit control surface when the model suggests actions.

options.label: LLM for every task
options.explanation: Smaller deterministic or ML systems can be cheaper, faster, and easier to control.

options.label: One giant prompt
options.explanation: Long prompts can waste tokens, increase cost, and still fail without structure and evaluation.

options.label: Validate tool execution
options.explanation: Tool validation prevents unsafe or malformed calls and gives the system an explicit control surface when the model suggests actions.

options.label: Trust requested tools
options.explanation: Tool calls need validation, permissions, and error handling before execution.

quizQuestions.prompt: How do you keep multi-tool flows from becoming unstable?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Use bounded tool policies, deterministic fallbacks, and clear stop conditions with observability on every tool result.

options.label: Bounded tools and fallbacks
options.explanation: Use bounded tool policies, deterministic fallbacks, and clear stop conditions with observability on every tool result.

options.label: One giant prompt
options.explanation: Long prompts can waste tokens, increase cost, and still fail without structure and evaluation.

options.label: Trust requested tools
options.explanation: Tool calls need validation, permissions, and error handling before execution.

options.label: Free-form integrations
options.explanation: Automated systems need schemas and validation so outputs can be parsed reliably.

quizQuestions.prompt: What are three things you should track to evaluate an LLM feature.
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Track quality, latency, cost, and failure rates to decide if prompts and tooling are meeting product expectations.

options.label: Quality, latency, cost, failures
options.explanation: Track quality, latency, cost, and failure rates to decide if prompts and tooling are meeting product expectations.

options.label: Trust requested tools
options.explanation: Tool calls need validation, permissions, and error handling before execution.

options.label: Free-form integrations
options.explanation: Automated systems need schemas and validation so outputs can be parsed reliably.

options.label: Keep all context
options.explanation: Context should be summarized, filtered, and constrained to reduce noise, cost, and privacy risk.
